title: Quiz: Quantum Option Pricing
description: Test your understanding of quantum algorithms for option pricing

questions.question: What is the speedup provided by Quantum Amplitude Estimation over classical Monte Carlo?
questions.explanation: QAE provides quadratic speedup. To achieve accuracy ε, classical MC needs O(1/ε²) samples, while QAE needs only O(1/ε) quantum queries - a quadratic improvement.

questions.question: In quantum option pricing, what does the amplitude of a quantum state represent?

options.text: The square root of the probability of a price outcome

questions.explanation: In quantum computing, the amplitude of a state squared gives the probability. To encode probabilities, we load the square root of each probability as the amplitude.

questions.question: Why can't current quantum computers achieve practical advantage for option pricing?

options.text: Options are too simple to need quantum computing
Noise and limited qubits degrade the theoretical speedup
Classical computers are faster than any quantum computer

questions.explanation: Current NISQ devices have high error rates and limited qubits. The required circuit depths for practical problems exceed what can be reliably executed, and noise accumulates faster than the speedup is realized.

questions.question: What is the role of the Grover operator in quantum amplitude estimation?

options.text: To amplify the amplitude of marked states

questions.explanation: The Grover operator amplifies the amplitude of states marked by an oracle (e.g., in-the-money states). This amplification is key to the quantum speedup in amplitude estimation.

questions.question: Quantum amplitude estimation gives exact option prices, not estimates.
questions.explanation: False. QAE still produces estimates, not exact values. The quantum advantage is that it achieves a given accuracy with fewer operations than classical Monte Carlo, but results remain probabilistic.

questions.question: How does the number of qubits affect price discretization accuracy?

options.text: n qubits allow n price levels
n qubits allow n² price levels
Number of qubits doesn't affect discretization

questions.explanation: With n qubits, we can represent 2^n distinct states, allowing us to discretize the price distribution into 2^n levels. More qubits mean finer discretization.

questions.question: What is a hybrid classical-quantum approach to option pricing?

options.text: Running the same calculation on both classical and quantum computers
Using classical preprocessing with quantum core computation
Using quantum computers only for data storage

questions.explanation: Hybrid approaches use classical computers for preprocessing (data preparation, dimension reduction) and postprocessing, while leveraging quantum computation for the core calculation where quantum speedup applies.

questions.question: Which are challenges for quantum option pricing? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Loading probability distributions into quantum states
Deep circuits required for amplitude estimation

questions.explanation: Loading distributions, deep circuit requirements, and quantum noise are all real challenges. Options are not inherently quantum - they're classical financial instruments; we're using quantum computers to price them faster.
